title: TPU v1 · Hardware/Software Partitioning: Harden Matrix Math, Leave Complex Control to the Host
oneLine: TPU is a decisive example of functional partitioning: harden only the most valuable, regular inference operations—matrix multiplication, activation, and normalization—and leave complex control and scheduling to host software.

legend.name: Host software (dashed)
legend.desc: Control flow, scheduling, operator orchestration, loops, and branches are complex but not performance hotspots. Leave them to the CPU host and TensorFlow/XLA instead of spending silicon on equivalent logic.

legend.name: MXU matrix unit (hardened core)
legend.desc: Matrix multiplication is the inference hotspot. Hardening it as a 256×256 systolic array is the central partitioning decision, with area and power prioritized for this block.

legend.name: Activation / pooling / normalization
legend.desc: A few frequent, regular elementwise operations beyond matrix multiplication also merit fixed-function units placed near the array output to avoid extra data movement.

legend.name: Unified Buffer (on-chip data hub)
legend.desc: A 24 MiB software-managed scratchpad keeps activations and intermediates on chip so fixed-function blocks can hand data off locally instead of repeatedly accessing DDR3.

legend.name: Weight FIFO / DDR3 (supply boundary)
legend.desc: The weight path is separated: DDR3 stores weights and the Weight FIFO prefetches them into the array, decoupling weight delivery from matrix computation.

steps.cap: ① Draw the boundary: divide functions into fixed hardware worth hardening (MXU, activation, normalization; solid) and complex control best left in software (dashed).
② Define the interface: the host issues only about a dozen CISC instructions over PCIe—load weights, multiply matrices, activate, write back—while the chip avoids complex control flow.
③ Separate supply: DDR3 → Weight FIFO prefetch → array decouples fetching weights from matrix multiplication.
④ Chain the datapath: array computation → partial-sum accumulators → activation, pooling, and normalization, connected through clear one-way interfaces.
⑤ Close the loop: write results to the Unified Buffer as the next layer's inputs; the on-chip hub circulates data while every module has one clear role.

before: Define the partitioning question: Which functions belong in hardware and which in software? TPU hardens only inference hotspots and delegates everything else to the host.

Decisions to make: The hardware/software boundary, the set of fixed-function blocks, block interfaces and data formats, instruction abstraction (fine-grained RISC or coarse-grained CISC), and whether control resides on chip or in the host.
Required inputs: The workload profile (§3.1)—especially which operators dominate runtime—the regularity of each operator, and the TensorFlow/XLA ecosystem into which the design must compile.
Hard constraints: Area and power limit how much can be hardened; changing models demand flexibility; and host bandwidth and latency determine whether control can be offloaded safely.
during: Set the partitioning rule: Harden functions that are both hot and regular; keep complex, fast-changing behavior in software. The core tradeoff is specialization for efficiency versus generality for flexibility.
Make matrix multiplication central: GEMM dominates inference time, so harden it as a 256×256 MXU and give it first claim on area and power. Every other block supports this core.

Add only a few specialized units: Activation, pooling, and normalization are frequent, regular elementwise operations. Small fixed-function units beside the array output process them locally and avoid round-trip data movement.

Delegate complex control to the host: Loops, branches, scheduling, and tensor orchestration are complex but not hotspots. A host-driven design sends about a dozen CISC instructions over PCIe, saving control area for MACs.

Choose coarse instruction semantics: With host control and long-running array operations, coarse CISC commands such as “matrix multiply” and “read weights” reduce instruction bandwidth and host overhead relative to fine-grained RISC.

Define clean module interfaces: Give DDR3/Weight FIFO, Unified Buffer, and accumulators explicit responsibilities for weights, activations/intermediates, and partial sums. Connect them with simple one-way dataflow interfaces.

result.label: Instruction set
result.value: Only about a dozen CISC instructions

after: Validate the partition: Run representative workloads and confirm that hardened blocks consume most execution time while host software and PCIe do not become bottlenecks. Move the boundary if they do.
Propagate block requirements: Specify the MXU for compute (§3.3), Unified Buffer capacity for memory (§3.4), delivery cadence for dataflow (§3.6), and an initial CISC control interface.
Freeze inter-block contracts: Document data formats, handshakes, and timing boundaries so teams can develop microarchitectures in parallel against stable interfaces.
Review flexibility headroom: Ask whether future operators can remain in host software or require another fixed-function unit, and use that answer to guide the product family.
Plan block-level verification and handoff: Define verification for every fixed-function block, performance-counter hooks, and co-verification with the TensorFlow/XLA software stack.
source: In-Datacenter Performance Analysis of a TPU, Jouppi et al., ISCA 2017. Facts disclosed in the paper are distinguished from illustrative examples and standard industry practice; the latter are not presented as disclosed TPU v1 implementation details.
